title: Temper rolling fluids
abstract: ABSTRACT The invention provides a composition for application to a metal surface, the composition comprising an inorganic nitrite, a benzoate and a carbonate in aqueous solution. The composition may advantageously be used as a lubricant or coolant during metal working, for example temper rolling, and for protection of coil or sheet packs of steel against corrosion.
description:
l~lLS~ This invention relates to a fluid composition suitable for application to a metal surface. The composition may advantageously be used as a lubricant or coolant during metal working or for protection of coil or sheet packs of steel against corrosion. The invention further relates to methods of use of the fluid composition. During certain metal working operations it is usual to supply a fluid to the surface of the metal to be worked to reduce friction and also to cool 'he work piece. In a number of cases, for example, during temper rolling and high speed cutting of steel, aqueous fluids ~re preferred on the basis of their cleaning action. The present invention will herein be described with particular reference to the exampl~ of temper rolling fluids but will be understood to be applicable to other aqueous metal working fluids such as cuttincl fluids. In the past temper rolLing r ~ cls have generaLly contained a nitrite salt to inhibit corrosion and one or more organic amine~ compoun(-ls, ~or examl)Le triethanolamine, to serve as a secondary corrosion inhibitor, .~nc1 to act as an ion complexant. Such fluicls sufler frc)m a nulnber of clisadvantages, including: 1. A tendency to foam, with consequent problems during metal working. 2. A tendency to hygroscopicity resulting in a complaint known as "wet pack" in respect of coil or sheet packs of steel which manifests itself in an unsightly - 2 - l~lS~ appearance of the steel surface and which i5 detrimental to paintability. 3. Complaints relating to v~elder tip life by users of the steel. 4. Significant problems in waste disposal and water treatment at rolling mills, caused by the inclusion in such fluids of amine compounds. 5. Because of the problems in pollution control which arise from previously used fluids, it has been practised to recirculate rolling fluids and this in turn has given rise to problems with corrosion inhibition, product cleanliness and degradation of the fluid. It is an object of the present invention to provide a fluid the use of which avoids or at least ameliorates some or all of those problems. The presenl. invention provides a rolling fluid which avoids most Or the disadvantages previously described while having sllbstantially similar or superior performance to known rolling fluids in most other respects. Mor:e particularly, com~ositions according to the present invention do not contain alllines and aEter use are more acceptabLe Ior waste dischargl? into thc cnvironment either with OL without waste treatment. The composition possesses a mild cleaning action and results in more effective cleaning of steel strip during temper rolling and less susceptibility to "wet pack" Eormation in comparison with previously used fluids, resulting in improved paintability of the steel surface. Composition according to the invention may be made suitable for use with steel intended for food grade application. ~olling fluids
115~ according to preferred embodiments are less susceptible to excessive foaming during use than previously used fluids and may be made at lower raw materials cost per unit volume and with lower waste discharge pollution loadings. The fluids may be applied at lower surface loadings than has been usual with fluids hitherto in use and inhibit corrosion in steel after unpacking when the steel is exposed to high humidity. According to a first aspect t:he invention consists in a composition for application to a metal surface comprising: an inorganic nitrite a benzoate a carbonate dissolved in aqueous solution. According to a second aspect the invention consists in a composition according to the ~irst ,~spect further comprising a glycol or polyol. According to a third aspect l:h-~ invention consists in a composition according to the first: or second aspect ~urther comprising a surfactant. ; 20 Accordiny to a ~ourth aspect t:~le invention consists in the method oE treatin(3 steel by aE~p1ying to the surface thereof a solution according to any one of the preceding aspects. Preferred embodiments of the invention will now be described with reference to the accompanying examples. A preferred embodiment of the invention employs as a concentrate a solution in water having the composition shown in Table 1. ~L~56i~34~ _ble 1 ComponentConcentration in concentrate (g/L) Sodium nitrite 120 Sodium carbonate 20 - 40 Sodium benzoate 10 Ethylene glycol 45 Fatty Alcohol Ethoxylate0.1 When intended for use as a temper rolling fluid the concentrate is typically diluted with water to a 5~ V/V working solution although working solutions of 2% V/V are effective. Formulations having concentrat;ons in the range shown in Table 2 have been found to be effective. Table 2 ComponentCollcentration in c~ncentrate (~/L) Sodium nitrite 108 - 132 Sodium carbonate 18 - 44 Sodium benzoate 9 - 11 Ethylene glycol 40 - 50 Fatty Alcohol ]3thoxylate 0.09 - 0.11 OE the components shown in Tables 1 and 2 only a nitrite, a carbonate and a benzoaLe are essential. It is believecl that the sodium nitrite functions as a primary c~rrosion inhibitor. Other alkali metal nitrites, for example potassium nitrite, could be substituted for sodium nitrite and the sodium salt is selected primarily for economic reasons. The sodium carbonate and sodium benzoate,
 -- 5 -- function as a buffer system to maintain a pH in the concentrate in the range of from pl-l 8.5 to pH 11.5 and more preferably of pH 10.5 to pH 11.5 in the working solution. However it is believed that the sodium carbonate in addition functions as a weak corrosion inhibitor and also contributes to humectant control. Moreover the sodium benzoate appears also to function as a secondary corrosion inhibitor. Alkali metal carbonates or benzoates other than sodium salts may be used, for example potassium salts~ One or more glycol or polyol for example glycerol or ethyl,ene glycol are preferably included and are believed to serve as additional humectant controllers. A surfactant and preferably a fatty alcohol ethoxylate in the range of 5 to 20 parts per million, is desirably added in order to stabilize the surface l-ension characteristics of - the solution without causing exce;siive foaming oE the solution during its use. Alterna~ ely, higher concentrations oE low-foaming surl'actants can be employed up to 100 ppm. For preference the working solutir,~n (at 5% V/V on the concentrate) has a surface tension of from 40 - 50 dynes/cm. When the rolllng fluicl is to be applied to steels to be used in food grade applications thell desirably food grade or equivalent constituents should be used. The formulation does not include added organic nitrogen compounds such as amines. It will be understood that no particular care is taken to exclude trace quantities of organic nitrogen compounds which may occur as impurities in other components but such quantities would be negligible in commercially used grades of
 -- 6 -- the constituents employed. It will also be understood that while salts other than the sodium salt of the benzoate may be used, the sodium salt is strongly preferred in the case of temper rolling fluids not only because of economic considerations, but also because improved control of humectant properties is obtained. For preference the proportion of carbonate and benzoate, to each other and to the nitrite may be varied to achieve a buffering of the pH within the range pH 8.5 to pH 11.3 in the concentrate and more preferably to achieve buffering of the working solution at a pH 10.5 to pH 11.5. Elowever because each of those constituents contributes to the effectiveness of the solution in other respects tl-e concentration ratios of those components described in Tabl.e I are very highly preferred. - In use a working solution according to the invention is applied, for example by spray, preEerably in the temperature range of rom 20 to 40C to steel strip at the temper mill. By way of Lurther example, thcre will. bc? (~escribed the results of comparative trials in whi.ch a commercially available amine cont~ining rolling 1uid concentrate (Al) was used as a contro.L. A 5~ working so:lution (A2) was made tllerefrom by dilution with water in accordance with usual practice. A solution having a composition according to Table 1 was prepared (concentrate Bl). ~ 5~ working solution (B2) was made therefrom by dilution with water. Two 20 tonne steel coils were temper rolled in suceession on one day, the first (coil 1) using solution B2, and the seeond (eoil 2) using eontrol solution A2, as the temper rolling fluid. Both eoils were from the same annealing charge. Eaeh eoil was cut in two at the mill. One portion of each was retained for storage testing over 5 months. As far as praetieable on eaeh inspeetion eoils were sampled at mid eoil position and tested for surfaee loadings edge-to-edge. Sa~pling of eoils at the ShearLine immediately after rolling showed that even at this stage solution B2 aeeording to the invention appeared to produce a visibly eleaner surface than did eontrol solution ~2. Analysis of surfaee loadings (Table 3) showed that based on turbidity results solution (B2) was giving a product: approximately twiee as elean as when solution A2 was use(l. In samples treated with B2 aeeordLnc3 to the invention, surraee loaclings of sodium and nitrite were advantageously appro~ nately 30 to 50~ of loadings obtainecl with solution ~). The second inspection and sa~ )Ling was carried out at the Shearline two rnonths later. No evi(lence of corrosion except at ullprotected eoil edges could be seen and the eonditioll of both coils was agreed to be satisfactory. It was notieeable, however, that the coil passivated with solution B2 aceording to the invention had a uniform dry appearanee, whereas the eoil treated with eontrol solution A2 had a sliek appearanee whieh eould almost qualify as a "wet pack". soth coils were repacked for further storage. After a total of five months storage, the coils were again inspected and sampled at the ~]itter prior to oiling for sale. sOth coils were free of corrosion and the coil treated with solution B2 was still dry. The coil treated with solution A2 (control) appeared to have undergone absorption and desorption of moisture and the surface appeared to be much dirtier than the surface of the coil treated according to the invention. One anomaly which became apparent in the course of investigation was that the turbidity measurements on the coil treated with solution A2 decreased whereas those from the coil treated will solution B2 were reasonably stable. This is considered to be due to the absorption and desorption of moisture in the coil treated with solution A2 giving rise to a flocculating action with particulate matter; the resultant larger particles being more readily visible and the strip appearing dirtier. Paradoxically, however, cleanliness (as measured by turbidity) increases a; the larger particles scatter less light and hence a lower turbidity is recorded. A milL trial was run on 10 coils temper rolled using a 5~ working solution (~3). Re~;ults o~ sampling during this trial are shown in Table 5. 'rhe results ~rom the two expe~imental coils and the 10 full scale trials SllOW that very corlsistent low surface loadings are acllieved usin(~ solutions according to the invention (Table 3). A comparison with results obtained during study of surface loadings when solution A2 was used indicate that lower and more consistent loadings are attained, with solutions of the invention.
 !94~ TABLE 3 Solution B2 according to the invention Sur~ace Loadings Sodium Range 1.2 - 3.2 mg/m2 Average 2.0 mg/m2 Nitrite Range 1.0 - 6.6 mg/m2 Average 3.1 mg/m2 Ethylene/glycol Range 0.5 - 2.6 mg/m2 Average 1.1 mg/m2 Benzoate Range 0.2 - 0.4 mg/m2 Averaye 0.2 mg/m2 Control Solution A2 According to prior art Sodium Range3.2 - 41 mg/m2 Average9.5 mg/m2 Nitrite Range4.3 - 35 mg/m2 Average10.1 mg/m2 Triethanolamine Range 2 - 60 mg/m2 Average11.2 mg/m2 Acetate Range1 - 28 mg/m2 Average8.4 mg/m2 These resuJ.ts indicate far better uniformity of surface loadings when solutions of the invenlion are used as well as lower surface loadings. The diluti.~n o~ solution Al to 2% levels in order to achieve lowel. sllrlace .loadings has been found to give unsatisfactory resul.~.; with the prior art concentrate. Some of the materials o the prescnt invention (e.g., ethylene glycol and benzoate) could barely be detected on the surface of product even with highly sensitive liqu:id chromatographic techniques sensitive to better than 1 ppm of benzoate. It has also been found that use of compositions according to the invention improve resistance of treated
 -- 10 -- steel to corrosion in high humidities. Samples of trial coils after 5 months storage were placed in a humidity cabinet of 93% R.H. overnight. It was found that the solution A2 treated metal had rusted whereas the solution B2 passivated metal was clean; in fact the solution B2 treated panel lasted for five days under the best condition before visible rust became evident. The test was repeated to confirrn the results. It is thought that the marked difference in corrosion performance can be explained as follows: After nitrite on the surface is exhausted by air access sheet rolled using control solution A2 will have a surface loading of acetate of approximately 2-5 mg/m2 in relation to the samples under discussion. It: has been shown that acetate is aggressive to steel and ha, high water absorption characteristics. After nitrite is e~hausted on sheet rolled with solutlon B2 small surface loadin~3s of carbonate and benzoate (0.2 - 1 mq/m2) will remain~ These components have low watt?r absorption cllaracteristics ancl in acldition benzoate appears to act as a general inhihitor. Benzoate is also apparelltly more effectivc as a~l inhibitor with free oxygen access. This corrosiotl supprc?ssion is oE particular advantage to end users oE unoiled coil such as cirulnstock manuEacturers. ~hilst stored in coil form, the passivation will be equivalent h7hen either prior art solution or those according to the invention are used. In loose sheet form during subsequent processing, superior corrosion resistance is exhibited by sheet treated according to the invention
 -- 11 -- particularly in high humidity conditions. In addition, the lower surface loadings and generally improved cleanliness results in improved paintability and paint adhesion. It has been found that sheet rolled with recirculated prior art solution is considerably dirtier than fresh solution, since particulate carbon in recirculated solution is redeposited on the strip during temper rolling. In the past approximately 60% of all product has been rolled with recirculated solution in order to m;nimize waste disposal due to problems with pollutant loadings. The solution of the invention need not be recirculated resulting in a substantial improvement in overall product cleanliness for unoiled and oiled uncoated product. In addition, it has been observed that the tendency towards emulsificalion of an oil, irrespective of its composition, is (:ar worse with recirculatecl solution. This appear~; to be clue to the presence of dirt in conjunction wil~11 the triethanolamine component oJ prior art solutionsand that tendency i5 avoided by use of non-recirculated solutions according to the invention. The present invention therefore provides: l. In-coil protection similar to that given b~ the prior art but at loadingc; of 1 - 3 mg/m2 instead o~ 5 - lO mg/m2 of nitrite. 2. Far better corrosion resistance, particularly with unoiled stock, after sheet is unpacked than is provided by the prior art. 3. The elimination of organic nitrogen components results ~lS~9~1 in fewer problems in water treatment of temper mill wastes, and enables the use of non-recirculated solution. 4. Since recirculation of solution is unnecessary a significant improvement of overall cleanliness of uncoated sheet is obtainable. 5. The use of lower surface loadings eliminates or substantially reduces wet pack occurrences and extends welder tip life.

claims:
THE EMBODIMENTS OF THE INVENTION IN WHICH AN EXCLUSIVE PROPERTY OR PRIVILEGE IS CLAIMED ARE DEFINED AS FOLLOWS:  1. A composition for application to a metal surface comprising: an inorganic nitrite, a benzoate, and a carbonate dissolved in aqueous solution. 
 2. A composition according to claim 1, further comprising a glycol or polyol. 
 3. A composition according to claim 1, further comprising a surfactant. 
 4. A composition according to claim 1, 2 or 3, wherein said inorganic nitrite is an alkali metal nitrite. 
 5. A composition according to claim 1, 2 or 3, wherein said benzoate is an alkali metal benzoate. 
 6. A composition according to claim 1, 2 or 3, wherein said carbonate is an alkali metal carbonate. 
 7. A composition according to claim 1, 2 or 3, wherein said inorganic nitrite is sodium nitrite. 
 8. A composition according to claim 1, 2 or 3, wherein said benzoate is sodium benzoate. 
 9. A composition according to claim 1, 2 or 3 wherein said carbonate is sodium carbonate. 

 10. A composition according to claim 1, 2 or 3, further comprising ethylene glycol or another polyol. 
 11. A composition according to claim 1, 2 or 3, further comprising a fatty acid ethoxylate. 
 12. A composition according to claim 1, 2 or 3, wherein the ratio of concentration of the carbonate to benzoate is selected to buffer the solution of a pH of from pH 8.0 to pH 11.5. 
 13. A composition according to claim 1, 2 or 3, wherein the carbonate is sodium carbonate, the benzoate is sodium benzoate, and the ratio by weight of sodium carbonate to sodium benzoate is in the range 2:1 to 4:1. 
 14. A composition according to claim 1, wherein the nitrite is sodium nitrite and the ratio by weight of sodium nitrite to sodium benzoate is in the range of from 9:1 to 15:1. 
 15. A composition according to claim 14, wherein the ratio by weight of sodium nitrite to sodium benzoate is in the range 6:1 to 12:1. 
 16. A composition according to claim 1, comprising less than 132 g/l of sodium nitrite. 
 17. A composition according to claim 1 comprising as solute per litre of solution of no more than 132 grams of sodium nitrite no more than to 11 grams of sodium benzoate no more than 44 grams of sodium carbonate no more than 50 grams of ethylene glycol no more than 0.11 grams of a surfactant. 
 18. A composition according to claim 17 when diluted substantially to 5% V/V with water and having a pH of 

 10.5 to 11.5 and a surface tension of 40-50 dynes/cm. 
 19. The method of treating steel comprising the steps of applying to the surface thereof a composition according to claim 1. 
 20. The method according to claim 19, when said composi- tion is applied so as to result in a surface loading of 1.0 to to 6.6 mg nitrite per meter of steel surface. 
 21. The method according to claim 19 or 20, wherein said composition is applied by being sprayed onto said surface. 
 22. The method according to claim 19, wherein said composition is applied to said steel prior to or during metal working as a lubricant or coolant. 
 23. The method according to claim 19, wherein said composition is applied to said steel prior to or during temper rolling as a temper rolling fluid.
